questions.subject: Polity
questions.prompt: The 'principle of substantive equality' was recently invoked by the Supreme Court in the context of digital transformation. This principle implies that:

options.text: Digital transformation must be both inclusive and equitable for all, including vulnerable groups.
The State must provide free internet hardware to every citizen under Article 14.
E-governance systems should be replaced by physical delivery to ensure equality.
Digital KYC should be made mandatory for all citizens without any exceptions.

questions.explanation: By invoking the 'principle of substantive equality', the Court emphasized that digital transformation must be inclusive and equitable, particularly ensuring that the eKYC process is accessible to persons with disabilities.
questions.takeaway: Substantive equality in the digital age requires proactive measures to make technology accessible to marginalized groups.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Invoking ‘principle of substantive equality’: Digital transformation must be both inclusive and equitable. ... Under Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016, Court issued twenty directions to make eKYC process accessible to them.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.prompt: The Supreme Court recently invoked the 'principle of substantive equality' in the context of digital transformation. Which of the following actions best illustrates the application of this principle according to the court?

options.text: Ensuring that all citizens have the same high-speed bandwidth regardless of their geographical location.
Revising digital KYC norms to accommodate individuals with facial disfiguration or visual impairment.
Mandating that all government services be moved exclusively to digital platforms to ensure uniformity.
Providing free digital devices to all students enrolled in government-run educational institutions.

questions.explanation: In the Amar Jain judgment, the Court invoked the 'principle of substantive equality' to ensure that digital transformation is inclusive. This was specifically applied by directing the revision of digital KYC norms to enable persons with facial disfiguration (due to acid attacks) or visual impairment to access banking and e-governance services, acknowledging that a 'one-size-fits-all' digital approach might exclude vulnerable groups.
questions.takeaway: Substantive equality in the digital realm requires proactive adjustments to infrastructure and norms to ensure accessibility for persons with disabilities.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Invoking ‘principle of substantive equality’: Digital transformation must be both inclusive and equitable. ... Directed to revise digital KYC norms: To enable persons with facial disfiguration due to acid attacks or visual impairment to access banking and e-governance services.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.prompt: Compare the judicial approach in Sabu Mathew George v. Union of India (2017) and Anuradha Bhasin v. Union of India (2020) regarding internet governance. Which of the following statements is correct?

options.text: Both cases primarily dealt with the suspension of internet services during civil unrest.
The Sabu Mathew George case focused on restricting specific illegal content without infringing on the general right to information.
The Anuradha Bhasin case was the first to declare that digital access is a part of the right to life under Article 21.
Both judgments held that the right to access the internet is a statutory right rather than a fundamental right.

questions.explanation: In Sabu Mathew George v. Union of India (2017), the Court ordered the blocking of prenatal sex determination advertisements but specifically noted this should be done without restricting general information access or free speech. In contrast, Anuradha Bhasin (2020) dealt with internet shutdowns and recognized access as a Fundamental Right under Article 19. The recognition under Article 21 came later in the Amar Jain case.
questions.takeaway: Judicial intervention in internet governance distinguishes between content-specific blocking (Sabu Mathew George) and the general right to access the medium (Anuradha Bhasin).
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Sabu Mathew George v. Union of India (2017): Ordered blocking of prenatal sex determination ads without restricting information access or free speech. Anuradha Bhasin v. Union of India (2020): Recognized Internet access as a Fundamental Right under Articles 19(1)(a) and 19(1)(g).
